Minecart for Niterra

Where AI actually fits in a field sales organization

You asked the right first question: how does AI make the sales team more efficient? Our answer runs on one 60-second voice note. It saves the rep time, gives managers visibility, reports back to your key accounts, and builds intelligence Niterra keeps.

Designed for members of

Four problems, one note

On our call you put it plainly: how do we use AI, where does it fit, and what do we do in the meantime? Here is the honest starting point. AI cannot be optimized on an organization without context, and today that context never gets captured. Information does not go in, so insight does not come out. Minecart fixes the going-in problem first, and everything else runs off the same 60-second voice note.

  • Reps save time. Walk out of the stop, press the button, talk for sixty seconds. The note does the admin: clean account notes, follow-ups turned into tracked activities, the next visit briefed. No forms, because we do not believe reps should fill out forms. They will not, and we build for that.
  • Managers get visibility. A daily digest per rep, a weekly rollup across the team, and an attention queue that surfaces only the notes that move the number. Opportunities appear as they escalate, pulled straight from the notes. That is how a $750,000 opportunity gets sized, assigned, and orchestrated with marketing pull-through, instead of staying an educated guess.
  • Stakeholders hear back. The same note, through a different lens: a readout to the O'Reilly team your rep rode with, a summary to the WD, an early signal to marketing. You choose who hears what, and nobody has to write a second report to make it happen.
  • Niterra keeps what it learns. The questions, objections, and customer language from the field come back as patterns that feed marketing, product, and strategy. The account knowledge stays when a rep moves on, and it loads into Salesforce on day one.

One capture, many lenses. You care about different data than Jacklyn does, and Japan cares about different data than either of you. Everyone gets their lens, off the same set of notes.

A normal approach vs the Tromml approach

A normal approach

The rep checks how the retailer is performing, goes out, visits accounts, runs demos.

A few notes get logged at the end of the day, if at all. Planning runs on memory.

Nobody can see how campaigns are landing. Marketing hears about needs a week ahead.

The retailer never hears what the field heard.

The Tromml approach

Same rep, same visits, plus sixty seconds of talking after each stop.

Briefed walking in: the last conversation, open promises, purchase history where the data is available.

Campaign progress is visible as it happens. The manager sees the week roll up. Marketing sees the objections and the customer's own words.

The retailer gets a readout of what the field is actually hearing.

The adoption secret is not discipline, it is self-interest. When reps do not feel like the logging makes them better at their job, they stop doing it, even when it is. So we made the logging make them better at their job: the note they take today is the briefing they get back tomorrow.

"I would really love for everyone to be able to see that."
Victoria, on the first-call and shelf intelligence your reps already gather on every stop. Today it stays with the rep. This is what routes it to you, to Jacklyn, and to the partners who need it.

How it works on a blitz

  1. Victoria walks into each stop prepared: a briefing with past visits, open promises, and purchase history where that data is available.
  2. She walks out and takes a sixty-second voice note. Minecart ties it to the account and pulls out the signal.
  3. Follow-ups become tracked activities. Real opportunities go to review, not into a sheet.
  4. Your report lands the same day: what happened, what needs attention, how the campaign is progressing.
  5. The knowledge stays with Niterra, and a cleaned-up readout can go back to the WD or the partner she rode with.

Once capture is this easy, reps take about five times more notes at twice the length, and roughly 90 percent of notes come in clean within the first 30 days. We will not pretend it is 100.

"This is the first time I've actually had a good grasp on what reps are doing every day."
Donovan, sales leader at PDI, a Federated WD on the distribution side of your counter

What this has to do with Salesforce

Salesforce is the right global initiative, and we are not here to compete with it. It will be a strong system of record. But a record system is a file storage system if nothing is getting activated on top of it, and a team that did not type into Sales-i will not type into Salesforce. Minecart is the action layer: it bridges the gap after Sales-i sunsets in September, builds the capture habit now, and hands Salesforce a year of clean, structured field intelligence on day one. We feed Salesforce. We do not replace it. And the intelligence stops walking out the door: today, when a rep leaves, their accounts start over from zero because the knowledge lived in their own system. Once it lives in Minecart, it stays with Niterra.

On the plumbing: Minecart syncs accounts, contacts, activities, and opportunities into Salesforce when you go live, on a schedule or through a direct integration, and your IT team chooses the connection method. It sits beside Kissflow rather than touching it: returns and credits keep their workflow, and field intelligence gets its own. Everything we capture is exportable at any time, so nothing is ever locked up.

Already running in your world

This is not a horizontal tool visiting the aftermarket, and it is not built for someone else’s org chart. Manufacturers like Snap-on and Dorman work with us on the marketing and voice-of-customer side. A rep agency is rolling it out across twenty reps specifically to report back to the manufacturers it represents, the same reporting-out job your team has with O’Reilly and your WDs. And we sit on both sides of your counter: distributors in program groups like Federated run it in the field, which means we already know how your channel talks. At one trade show last year, a team captured about 250 notes between handshakes and pulled roughly $9 million in pipeline out of them. That is the difference between a show you attended and a show you can measure.

"I never knew a single button could change my business so much."
Angelica Polly

Why this matters right now

Sales-i ends in September and Salesforce is roughly a year out. The new product lines are launching in exactly that window, and coils, sensors, and rotating electrical do not sell themselves the way plugs do. Every month without capture is context Salesforce will never get and plays that never ran. The teams that walk into a CRM rollout with the capture habit already built are the ones that get their money's worth from it.

Recommended pilot

  • Preload historical Sales-i data and accounts for 3 to 4 reps, so briefings are useful from day one.
  • 30 days of live field capture in Minecart, including any blitzes in the window.
  • Cleaned data exports back to Jacklyn, plus training to connect it to her CRM and put it to work with AI tools, the way we have done with manufacturers like Snap-on.
  • Reporting lenses for you, your managers, and marketing, available in the AI tools you already use, including Claude. We teach that too.
  • What it asks of your team: about an hour of onboarding per rep, then the sixty seconds a note takes. No laptops, no forms.

Your data stays in Niterra's own tenant on private servers and is never shared with public AI models. Pricing is a flat platform fee plus a per-user fee. No implementation consultants, no enterprise services contract, and nothing that looks like a CRM quote.

We will load a sample of your accounts and walk the rep view, the manager view, and the marketing view side by side.
